1: YHWH spoke to Moses — the opening revelation formula for the sexual prohibition code. The covenant framing of the entire chapter (Egypt/Canaan contrast, YHWH-self-identification) establishes that sexual ethics is a theological rather than merely social or biological matter. Christ's sexual ethics teaching maintains this covenant-theological grounding: 'Have you not read that he who created them from the beginning made them male and female, and said, “Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife”?' (Matthew 19:4-5). The Creator's design is the ground of the ethics, not cultural convention.
2: Ani YHWH Eloheichem — I am YHWH your God. The covenant-identity formula grounds the sexual ethics in who YHWH is, not merely in what he commands. Christ grounds his ethical teaching in the same way: 'I say to you' (Matthew 5:22,28,32,34,39,44) — the 'you have heard it said' becomes 'but I say to you,' positioning his own authority as the new legislative basis, the fulfillment of the Mosaic legislation.
3: You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt or as they do in the land of Canaan — the negative boundary-definition: Israel's ethics is not Egypt (slavery, law-without-grace) nor Canaan (freedom-without-law). Paul applies this same third-way logic to the church: 'you are not under law but under grace' (Romans 6:14-15) — not Egypt's servitude nor Canaan's license but the freedom of the sons of God who 'through the Spirit... put to death the deeds of the body' (Romans 8:13). Christ creates the third way that the Egypt/Canaan contrast could only gesture toward.
4: You shall follow my rules and keep my statutes and walk in them — the positive command after the negative boundary. Christ fulfills the positive command: 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them' (Matthew 5:17). He walks in the statutes perfectly, providing both the pattern of obedience and the atoning sacrifice for those who fail.
5: Va-chai bahem — the person who does them shall live by them. This verse is Paul's key citation for the law-covenant in contrast to the faith-covenant: 'The one who does them shall live by them' (Galatians 3:12, citing Leviticus 18:5 to show that the law operates on a doing-not-faith principle); and 'the righteousness based on the law... the person who does the commandments shall live by them' (Romans 10:5, contrasting with Leviticus 18:6's faith-righteousness). Christ fulfills Leviticus 18:5 by perfectly doing the commandments and thereby living — and then gives that life to those who have not done them, through faith rather than law-performance (Romans 10:9).
6: None of you shall approach any one of his close relatives to uncover nakedness — the foundational prohibition of the incest laws: she'er basar (flesh-kin) defines the protected zone of family relationships. Christ honors and protects these boundaries, establishing the family as the primary unit of society: 'Honor your father and mother' (Matthew 15:4); his teaching defends both marital fidelity (Matthew 5:27-32) and family obligations (Mark 7:9-13).
7: The nakedness of your father, which is the nakedness of your mother, you shall not uncover — the parent prohibition. Christ's deepest honoring of the parent-child boundary is in his own submission to Joseph and Mary: 'he went down with them and came to Nazareth and was submissive to them' (Luke 2:51); and his care for Mary from the cross: 'When Jesus saw his mother and the disciple whom he loved standing nearby, he said to his mother, “Woman, behold, your son!”' (John 19:26). He who is the Word incarnate submits to the family structure he created.
8: The nakedness of your father's wife — the stepmother prohibition, cited by Paul as the Corinthian violation: 'it is actually reported that there is sexual immorality among you, and of a kind that is not tolerated even among pagans, for a man has his father's wife' (1 Corinthians 5:1). Paul's appeal to the Levitical standard confirms that the sexual ethics of Lev 18 continues to govern the new covenant community's practice, even as the ceremonial and sacrificial law has been fulfilled and transformed.
9: The nakedness of your sister — the sibling prohibition. Christ establishes new family categories that transcend but do not replace biological family: 'Who are my mother and my brothers?... Whoever does the will of God, he is my brother and sister and mother' (Mark 3:33,35). The redefinition of family around covenant rather than biology does not dissolve the biological family's protective boundaries but situates them within the larger covenant community.
10: The nakedness of your son's daughter or of your daughter's daughter — the grandchild prohibition based on flesh-identity. The generational extension of kinship protection points to Christ's concern for children and the vulnerable: 'Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them' (Mark 10:14); 'Whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to sin, it would be better for him to have a great millstone fastened around his neck and to be drowned in the depth of the sea' (Matthew 18:6). The protective framework for the most vulnerable family members is intensified, not relaxed, in Christ's teaching.
11: The nakedness of your father's wife's daughter — the stepsister prohibition based on relational (not biological) kinship. The equal weight of relational and biological kinship in the incest prohibitions confirms that family structures created by covenant (marriage) are as real as those created by birth. Christ honors covenant-created relationships: the church is his bride (Ephesians 5:25-27), created by covenant rather than biology, with all the full reality and obligation of the most intimate family bond.
12: The nakedness of your father's sister — the paternal aunt prohibition. The comprehensive coverage of extended family in the incest laws reflects the biblical understanding that the family is the fundamental social unit whose integrity must be protected at every generational level. Christ's coming does not dissolve these structures: 'do not think that I have come to abolish' (Matthew 5:17) — the creational and covenantal family structures are not abolished but fulfilled and purified in the new creation.
13: The nakedness of your mother's sister — the maternal aunt prohibition, symmetrical with the paternal. The bilateral symmetry of the aunt prohibitions (paternal and maternal equally protected) reflects the equal dignity of both sides of the family structure. Christ's new covenant creates a bilateral covenant family: both Jew and Gentile, male and female, slave and free are equally included — 'there is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28).
14: The nakedness of your father's brother — the uncle relationship protected by extension. Paul uses family relationship language for the church: 'Do not rebuke an older man but encourage him as you would a father, younger men as brothers, older women as mothers, younger women as sisters, in all purity' (1 Timothy 5:1-2). The family-relationship structure of Lev 18 is the template for the church's relational ethics: all the protective respect owed to family members is owed to fellow church members.
15: The nakedness of your daughter-in-law — the daughter-in-law prohibition. The family-by-marriage is as protected as the family-by-blood. Christ's treatment of the Samaritan woman (John 4) and the widow of Nain (Luke 7:11-15) shows the extension of covenant protection to women in vulnerable family positions (socially excluded, widowed). He honors and protects precisely those whom the family protection laws of Lev 18 were designed to cover.
16: The nakedness of your brother's wife — the brother's wife prohibition (with levirate exception). John the Baptist's rebuke of Herod Antipas for this violation (Mark 6:17-18) shows that the Lev 18 sexual ethics were applied in NT prophetic ministry as the binding standard for covenant behavior. John's willingness to die for this rebuke (Mark 6:27-28) confirms that the sexual ethics of the Holiness Code were not understood as merely Mosaic legislation superseded by the new covenant but as creational-covenantal standards whose prophetic enforcement was continuous.
17: A woman and her daughter — the three-generation prohibition of simultaneous relationships with a woman and her daughter/granddaughter. The most severe penalty in Leviticus 20:14 (burning) matches the severity of the term here: zimmah (deliberate depravity). Christ's eschatological judgment language reserves its most severe imagery for those who harm the most vulnerable: 'it would be better for him to have a great millstone fastened around his neck and to be drowned' (Matthew 18:6) — the triple-generation violation that exploits the structures of family trust deserves the triple-generation's protective curse.
18: A woman as a rival wife to her sister — the rival-sisters prohibition. Christ resolves the tensions created by the polygamy that the Levitical legislation regulated without endorsing: 'from the beginning it was not so' (Matthew 19:8), appealing to the creation norm (Genesis 2:24) as the standard that the Mosaic legislation partially accommodated but did not establish. Christ returns the marriage ethics to its pre-fallen standard.
19: Do not approach a woman in her menstrual uncleanness — the niddah prohibition. The woman with a twelve-year flow of blood (Mark 5:25-34) — living under continuous niddah impurity — approaches Christ and is healed; he declares her healed and at peace rather than rebuking the touch. Christ does not abolish the niddah legislation but fulfills its trajectory: his healing of her chronic discharge demonstrates his power over the very category of impurity the legislation addressed.
20: You shall not lie with your neighbor's wife — the adultery prohibition. Christ intensifies this: 'everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart' (Matthew 5:28). The Levitical prohibition of the outward act is brought to bear on the inward disposition; Christ applies the law at its deepest level, fulfilling rather than relaxing the marital-covenant protection of Leviticus 18:20.
21: You shall not give any of your children to Molech — child sacrifice in the heart of the sexual prohibition chapter. Christ's protection of children is the antitype of the Molech prohibition: 'Let the children come to me; do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of God' (Mark 10:14). The same passage where Christ blesses children (Mark 10:13-16) contrasts directly with the Molech sacrifice of children: one is the perversion of parenthood (giving children to death), the other its fulfillment (bringing children to the living God).
22: You shall not lie with a male as with a woman — the prohibition placed in the list of violations that are to'evah (abomination) to YHWH. Paul's treatment in Romans 1:26-27 situates this prohibition within the broader framework of creation-order violations that result from suppressing the truth of God: 'they exchanged natural relations for those that are contrary to nature.' The to'evah category of Lev 18 is applied in the new covenant not as a ceremonial violation but as a creation-order deviation addressed through the gospel's transforming power.
23: You shall not lie with any animal — the bestiality prohibition; tevel (confusion of kinds, species-boundary violation). The creation order that the bestiality prohibition protects ('every creature according to its kind,' Gen 1) is the same creation order that Christ's parousia will restore: 'the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God' (Romans 8:21). The tevel that violated created categories will be reversed when all creation is renewed in Christ.
24: Do not make yourselves unclean by any of these things — the closing frame: the nations were expelled for these violations. Christ gathers from all nations the 'clean' community — not those who never violated these laws but those who have been washed: 'But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ' (1 Corinthians 6:11). The nations' expulsion through defilement is answered by the nations' gathering through purification in Christ.
25: The land became unclean, so I punished its iniquity upon it, and the land vomited out its inhabitants — vataqe ha-aretz et-yoshveiha. Christ bears the land's vomiting upon himself: he is executed outside the city (the land vomits him out), cursed on the tree (Galatians 3:13: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, “Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree”'), and buried in the earth that had vomited its inhabitants. He enters the curse-trajectory of the land to reverse it from within.
26: You shall keep my statutes and my rules and do none of these abominations, either the native or the stranger — the universal covenant standard for all who live in the land. In Christ, the sojourner-Gentile and the native-Israelite are equally incorporated into the covenant people: 'he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility' (Ephesians 2:14). The equal application of the Holiness Code to native and sojourner is fulfilled in the equal membership of Jew and Gentile in the one body of Christ.
27: For the people of the land who were before you did all of these abominations — the Canaanite violations are the historical ground for the expulsion. Christ does not dismiss this history but recapitulates it in reverse: where Canaan's sin produced expulsion, his bearing of all human sin (including Canaan's) in the cross turns expulsion into restoration. 'In Christ God was reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them' (2 Corinthians 5:19) — the world's Canaanite-magnitude sin is not held against those in Christ.
28: Lest the land vomit you out when you make it unclean, as it vomited out the nation that was before you — the warning applied to Israel: the same covenant principle that expelled Canaan will expel Israel if they follow the same path. History confirmed this (the Babylonian exile). Christ absorbs the land-vomiting curse so that his people will not be vomited out: 'whoever feeds on my flesh and drinks my blood abides in me, and I in him' (John 6:56). To abide in Christ is to be kept in the land; to be ejected from Christ is the only true exile.
29: Everyone who does any of these abominations shall be cut off from among his people — karet for the entire violation-list. Christ's death is the ultimate karet — cut off from the land of the living (Isaiah 53:8: nigzar me-eretz hayyim, cut off from the land of the living) — borne in the place of those who deserved the cutting off. 'The punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed' (Isaiah 53:5) — the karet that belonged to the violators falls on the one who bore their violations.
30: Ani YHWH — the final covenant self-identification closes the chapter where it began. The chapter's architecture (Leviticus 18:2: ani YHWH Eloheichem; Leviticus 18:30: ani YHWH) frames all the sexual ethics within the divine identity. Christ's closing statement in the Sermon on the Mount mirrors this: 'everyone then who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock' (Matthew 7:24). The divine-identity ground of Lev 18's ethics is fulfilled in the 'I say to you' authority of the one who is 'greater than the temple' (Matthew 12:6) and 'Lord of the Sabbath' (Matthew 12:8).
